phrasalVerbs.definition: To combine to produce a particular result or effect; to amount to a total.
examples: Small savings add up to significant amounts over time.
These expenses add up to more than our monthly budget.
All the evidence adds up to a clear conclusion.

phrasalVerbs.definition: To stop something from having any effect; to neutralise.
examples: The gains were cancelled out by unexpected losses.
His efforts to save money were cancelled out by rising prices.
The positive and negative factors tend to cancel out.

phrasalVerbs.definition: To reach a particular number or amount; to accumulate.
examples: The company has clocked up record profits this year.
She's clocked up over a million air miles.
They've clocked up considerable debts over the years.

phrasalVerbs.definition: If people club together, each of them gives some money so all the money collected can be used to buy something.
examples: We clubbed together to buy her a retirement gift.
The neighbours clubbed together to fund the street party.
Let's club together and get him something really special.

phrasalVerbs.definition: To reduce the amount of something, especially money that you spend.
examples: We've had to cut back on luxury items this year.
The company is cutting back on staff training budgets.
They're cutting back their spending after the recession.

phrasalVerbs.definition: To get much larger; to accumulate to a significant amount.
examples: Credit card bills can mount up quickly if you're not careful.
The repair costs started to mount up alarmingly.
Evidence against the suspect continued to mount up.

phrasalVerbs.definition: To spend or pay money, especially a lot of money.
examples: We've paid out a fortune on home improvements.
The insurance company refused to pay out on the claim.
They're paying out millions in compensation.

phrasalVerbs.definition: To help someone to get to the end of a difficult period of time, especially by giving them money until they can get some more.
examples: Could you lend me £50 to tide me over until payday?
The emergency fund will tide us over for a few months.
Her parents helped tide her over during unemployment.

fillInExercises.sentence: All the small purchases ___ a surprisingly large amount.
fillInExercises.explanation: 'Add up to' means to amount to a total.

fillInExercises.sentence: The tax refund was ___ out by the car repair bill.
fillInExercises.explanation: 'Cancel out' means to neutralise or negate the effect of something.

fillInExercises.sentence: The department has ___ impressive sales figures this quarter.
fillInExercises.explanation: 'Clock up' means to achieve or accumulate a particular amount.

fillInExercises.sentence: We all ___ together to buy the manager a farewell present.
fillInExercises.explanation: 'Club together' means to pool money for a shared purchase.

fillInExercises.sentence: Due to the recession, we've had to ___ on non-essential expenses.
fillInExercises.explanation: 'Cut back on' means to reduce spending or consumption.

fillInExercises.sentence: The unpaid bills continued to ___ up throughout the year.
fillInExercises.explanation: 'Mount up' means to accumulate to a large amount.

fillInExercises.sentence: The insurance company finally ___ the full claim amount.
fillInExercises.explanation: 'Pay out' means to give out money, usually a large sum.

fillInExercises.sentence: This loan should ___ us over until the investment matures.
fillInExercises.explanation: 'Tide over' means to help someone through a difficult financial period.

lines.speaker: Priya
lines.text: Our monthly expenses [add up to|add up to] more than we earn.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: 'Add up to' means to amount to a total.
explanationContent.vocabularyFocus: *add up to* — To combine to produce a particular result or effect; to amount to a total.
explanationContent.sentenceBreakdown: Our monthly expenses add up to more than we earn.
explanationContent.commonMistake: Do not confuse this with other items from the lesson — here you need *add up to* (*add up to*).

wrongOptions.cancel out: *cancel out* (To stop something from having any effect) does not match the meaning needed in this line.
wrongOptions.clock up: *clock up* (To reach a particular number or amount) does not match the meaning needed in this line.

wrongOptions.mount up: *mount up* (To get much larger) does not match the meaning needed in this line.
wrongOptions.pay out: *pay out* (To spend or pay money, especially a lot of money) does not match the meaning needed in this line.
wrongOptions.tide over: *tide over* (To help someone to get to the end of a difficult period of time, especially by giving them money until they can get some more) does not match the meaning needed in this line.

explanationContent.usefulTip: In conversation, *add up to* is often used in informal spoken English.
